videos.title: Duck For Hire
videos.description:
This episode of Wonderful World of Disney includes excerpts from a number of vintage cartoons including Lucky Number, Straight Shooters, Truant Officer Donald, Lighthouse Keeping, Bellboy Donald, and All in a Nutshell. It features new linking animation with J. Audubon Woodlore and Donald Duck.

The episode originally aired in 1957; this VHS transfer is from a syndicated airing in Winter 1987.

videos.title: Horror Movie Trailers, 1970s
videos.description: A series of horror movie trailer television ads from the 1970s and 1980s. The opening segment is black video with a dub of a brief recording of a drive-in intermission, then a scratch animated title. This segment originally appeared as a film set shown at The Escape' Drive-in, during a 1989 screening.

videos.title: The art of video art
videos.description: Using footage from the Prelinger archives, The art of video art uses vintage TV commercials to create a visually retro essay about the ontology of video art, the art of the televisual age.
videos.creator: Lobo Pasolini

videos.title: The All New Dating Game (1987 Broadcast, WABC-TV 7 NY, Complete with Commercials)
videos.description:
A complete VHS recording of The All New Dating Game as aired on WABC-TV Channel 7 New York circa 1987, presented with original commercials, bumpers, and network promos fully intact.

This version of The Dating Game was part of the 1986–1989 reboot hosted by Elaine Joyce, featuring the classic format of three unseen suitors (or bachelorettes) answering quirky questions to win a date. The show retained its iconic charm while embracing the bold fashion, pastel lighting, and playful energy of the late '80s.

This broadcast includes:

  • 📺 Local and national 1987 commercials

  • 🎥 Promos for other ABC7 programming

  • 🕹️ Bumpers, transitions, and tape glitches

  • 📼 Classic VHS tracking noise and analog artifacts

Captured straight from a home-recorded VHS tape, this upload is part of the Sleepover Trading Company digital preservation project, celebrating ephemeral media and forgotten TV moments.

📌 Notes:

  • Exact airdate unknown, but broadcast likely in late 1987

  • Sourced from a standard SP-speed VHS tape recorded in the NYC area

  • Minor tape wear and tracking noise present throughout

  • Elaine Joyce served as host during this era of The All New Dating Game

🕒 Timestamps (Approx.):

00:00 – Opening bumpers
00:25 – Show intro
01:00 – Segment 1
10:15 – Commercial break (includes local NYC ads)
… [Add more if you'd like – optional for now]

🎞️ Credits:

Host: Elaine Joyce
Production: Chuck Barris Productions
Broadcast on: WABC-TV 7, New York
Preserved by: Sleepover Trading Company – sleepovertrading.com
videos.creator: Sleepover Trading Co

videos.title: WBNG-TV "Action News" from February 5, 1986 (Partial) With Original Commercials
videos.description:
CAPTURE LINEAGE AND TECHNICAL DETAILS ARE BELOW. PLEASE READ THIS TEXT IN ITS ENTIRETY.

This video captures a segment of an Action News broadcast from WBNG-TV Channel 12, the CBS affiliate in Binghamton, NY, likely from February 5, 1986. The broadcast features a mix of national and local news, along with local commercials and a public service announcement, offering a snapshot of regional television from the mid-1980s.

The news segment is anchored by Jim Mathews, Mitch Gross, and Bob Buchanan, with reports from Frank Sesno and Liz Cormelline. Major national stories covered include the Space Shuttle Challenger tragedy, which occurred just a week prior, and President Ronald Reagan's fifth State of the Union address delivered on February 4, 1986. During his address, Reagan notably quoted a line from the hit movie Back to the Future, stating, "Where we're going, we don't need roads." He also reaffirmed commitments to the space program, national defense, social security, and avoiding tax increases, while calling for welfare reform. Other news items include the decision to keep the 49th Fighter Interceptor Squadron at Griffiss Air Force Base in Rome, New York, and the Senate's approval of the $1.2 billion sale of the federally owned Conrail system to Norfolk Southern, with a graphic detailing the split votes of New York and Pennsylvania senators. A local report by Liz Cormelline focuses on school zone safety at the intersection of Route 7 and 12A near Chenango Valley High School in Chenango Bridge, where the speed limit was lowered to 45 mph.

Interspersed with the news are several commercials and a public service announcement. The PSA, funded by the New York State Department of Social Services, addresses domestic violence, featuring various women sharing their experiences and providing a 24-hour hotline number. Commercials include an advertisement for Avant-Garde Eyewear from "The Eye Guys" at "The OptiKal," showcasing various stylish glasses and sunglasses, and a "Store Crazy Sale" advertisement for Sears, highlighting discounts on clothing, home goods, and appliances. The broadcast also includes a station identification for WBNG-TV Channel 12, featuring the slogan "We've Got The Touch."

  • 00:10 [Commercial} The Law Offices of Ronald Benjamin (partial)
  • 00:13 [PSA] New York State Department of Social Services ("domestic violence hurts everyone")
  • 00:46 [Commercial] Avant-Garde Eyeware at The OptiKal (this was a small local business, the ad is probably a combination of a localizable ad from Avant-Garde Eyewear and a custom voiceover)
  • 01:16 [Commercial] Sears ("Store Crazy" sale, biggest store-wide sale of the year)
  • 01:27 [Station ID] WBNG-TV 12 ("We've got the touch on TV-12!")
  • 01:32 WBNG-TV Action News Open
  • 01:53 WBNG-TV Action News A-block (partial)

SEEKING SOUND FILMSTRIPS FOR PRESERVATION. In addition to saving select recordings on VHS, I am the only person on earth preserving American filmstrips. The media format is largely forgotten and very endangered. If you have any American filmstrips, or can rescue any filmstrips from a school, college, business, industry, or elsewhere, please contact me immediately at uncommon.ephemera@gmail.com. Don't know what a filmstrip is? Read: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Filmstrip

I AM AN INDEPENDENT MEDIA PRESERVATIONIST. If you enjoy my work please consider making a donation toward my operating expenses. Visit https://uncommonephemera.org/donate for links to Patreon, GoFundMe, and more.

STAY IN TOUCH. Join my Discord for announcements on new releases and live shows, discussion with other people interested in media preservation, or if you can help in any way.

For more information about Uncommon Ephemera, filmstrips, my media preservation processes, file specifications, contact information, and attribution information, please see the included “Read Me.txt” file.

CAPTURE LINEAGE. Items uploaded prior to January 1, 2025 were played on a JVC HR-S7500U VCR, connected via S-Video into a Panasonic DMR-ES15 for timebase correction and frame sync, connected via component to a BlackMagic Intensity Shuttle USB and captured uncompressed with BlackMagic Media Express at 59.94 fields per second interlaced at 10-bit color with uncompressed 24-bit audio at 48kHz. Items uploaded after January 1, 2025 were played on a JVC HR-S7500U, connected via S-Video into a Panasonic DMR-ES15 for timebase correction and frame sync, connected via component to a BlackMagic Analog to SDI Mini Converter, connected via SDI to a BlackMagic UltraRecorder 3G and captured uncompressed with BlackMagic Media Express at 59.94 fields per second interlaced at 10-bit color with uncompressed 24-bit audio at 48kHz. The video is encoded to the lossless FFV1 codec before uploading to the Internet Archive.

ANALOG VIDEO IS INTERLACED. If you use any part of this video in a derivative work in a non-interlaced format, please use the original Matroska (.MKV) video in this item and properly deinterlace it. This video has been captured in its native interlaced format and will look terrible if not properly deinterlaced before using. Your editing software may do this automatically; please refer to its documentation for more information.

DO NOT USE THE MP4 OR h.264 PREVIEW VIDEO FOR DERIVATIVE WORKS OR ARCHIVAL. The Internet Archive automatically creates a low-bitrate h.264 video for preview and watching at the top of this page. This video is for preview only and is not suitable for derivative works or archival. Please use the original Matroska (.MKV) video in this item. Please properly de-interlace the video file before using in a progressive-scan format such as internet video.

PLEASE ATTRIBUTE THE PRESERVATION OF THIS MEDIA. Media preservation takes money, time, and effort. If you use any part of this video in a derivative work, please attribute the preservation of this media to Uncommon Ephemera.

videos.title: tremulous-promo-II
videos.description:
Tremulous is a free, open source game that blends a team based FPS with elements of an RTS. Players can choose from 2 unique races, aliens and humans. Players on both teams are able to build working structures in-game like an RTS. These structures provide many functions, the most important being spawning. The designated builders must ensure there are spawn structures or other players will not be able to rejoin the game after death. Other structures provide automated base defense (to some degree), healing functions and much more... Player advancement is different depending on which team you are on. As a human, players are rewarded with credits for each alien kill. These credits may be used to purchase new weapons and upgrades from the "Armoury". The alien team advances quite differently. Upon killing a human foe, the alien is able to evolve into a new class. The more kills gained the more powerful the classes available. The overall objective behind Tremulous is to eliminate the opposing team. This is achieved by not only killing the opposing players but also removing their ability to respawn by destroying their spawn structures.
tremulous.net for more info
This is an amazing freeware game from the early to mid 2000s, that still has a small cult following in 2020.
videos.creator: Dark Legion group
